text:
Cox proportional hazards modeling of remission duration in AML patients, with interpretation of hazard ratios and treatment effects.

Tools: R, multinomial logistic regression, grouped summaries, probability modeling

Two R-based ANOVA studies examining teak tree growth under different planting methods and root lengths, and nitrogen fixation in clover under varying rhizobium strain and culture combinations.
Tools: R, two-way ANOVA, interaction plots, simple effects, Tukey HSD

Three R-based analyses: custom contrasts for age-group comparisons, transformed regression with interaction for onion yield, and MANOVA for wheat grain and straw across field rows and columns.
Tools: R, custom contrasts, linear modeling, Box-Cox transformation, interaction analysis, MANOVA, data visualization

title: About
text:
About this site

1 + 1

[1] 2

title: Multinomial Election and Snowfall Timing Analysis
text: This project combines two statistical analyses completed in R. The first uses multinomial logistic regression to examine how the proportion of Latin-American voters in a precinct relates to support for three candidates in a Chicago city election. The second analyzes snowfall timing in Halifax by identifying the most common and least common dates and months for snowfall events.

title: Multinomial Election and Snowfall Timing Analysis
text: This project combines two statistical analyses completed in R. The first uses multinomial logistic regression to examine how the proportion of Latin-American voters in a precinct relates to support for three candidates in a Chicago city election. The second analyzes snowfall timing in Halifax by identifying the most common and least common dates and months for snowfall events.

title: Multinomial Election and Snowfall Timing Analysis
section: Part 1: Multinomial Election Analysis in Chicago
text:
Part 1: Multinomial Election Analysis in Chicago
Because the response variable consists of three unordered candidate categories, multinomial logistic regression provides an appropriate way to model how vote probabilities change across precincts.

Data
The election dataset contains 27 precincts in Chicago’s 22nd Ward and includes:

latv: number of Latin-American voters in the precinct
nonlv: number of non-Latin-American voters
turnout: total number of voters
Garcia, Martinez, Yanez: votes cast for each candidate

A new variable, pct_latin, was created to represent the proportion of Latin-American voters in each precinct.


Methods

Calculated pct_latin as the proportion of Latin-American voters in each precinct
Reshaped candidate vote counts into long format
Fit a multinomial logistic regression model predicting candidate choice from pct_latin
Used vote totals as weights so the model reflected actual precinct-level vote counts
Compared the full model against an intercept-only model
Computed predicted probabilities for each candidate at selected values of pct_latin



Results
Model comparison showed that pct_latin had a highly significant effect on candidate choice. As the proportion of Latin-American voters increased, the predicted probability of voting for Garcia decreased, the probability of voting for Martinez increased, and the probability of voting for Yanez remained low and changed only slightly. These results suggest that precinct demographic composition was strongly associated with the distribution of votes across candidates. :contentReferenceoaicite:1

title: Multinomial Election and Snowfall Timing Analysis
text:
Part 2: Halifax Snowfall Timing Analysis
This section examines the timing of snowfall in Halifax by identifying which months and dates most often receive measurable snowfall and which are least likely to do so.

Methods

Extracted month and day from each snowfall date
Filtered observations to days with snowfall greater than zero
Counted snowfall occurrences by month and by calendar date
Identified the most frequent and least frequent snowfall timings



Results
The analysis showed that snowfall in Halifax is concentrated in winter months, with some dates and months appearing much more frequently than others. This provides a simple but effective example of how date-based data can be transformed and summarized to identify seasonal timing patterns.

title: Multinomial Election and Snowfall Timing Analysis
text:
Visualization
The original assignment included predicted probability outputs for the Chicago election model and summarized timing patterns for Halifax snowfall.
View full analysis and code (PDF)

title: Multinomial Election and Snowfall Timing Analysis
text:
Key Takeaways

Multinomial logistic regression is useful when the response has more than two unordered categories
Weighting matters when each row represents many observations rather than a single case
Date variables can be transformed into interpretable components such as month and day to study seasonal patterns
Predicted probabilities and grouped summaries make statistical results easier to interpret substantively

title: Regression and Logistic Modeling in R
text: This project combines two statistical modeling exercises completed in R. The first examines the relationship between roller weight and lawn depression using simple linear regression. The second uses logistic regression to evaluate how different dilution levels affect seed germination probabilities.

title: Regression and Logistic Modeling in R
text: This project combines two statistical modeling exercises completed in R. The first examines the relationship between roller weight and lawn depression using simple linear regression. The second uses logistic regression to evaluate how different dilution levels affect seed germination probabilities.

title: Regression and Logistic Modeling in R
section: Part 1: Linear Regression on Lawn Rolling
text:
Part 1: Linear Regression on Lawn Rolling
This analysis models the relationship between roller weight and the amount of lawn depression caused under dry conditions.

Methods

Created a scatterplot to assess the form of the relationship
Fit a linear regression model with depression as the response and weight as the predictor
Generated predicted values and confidence intervals for selected roller weights
Interpreted the fitted line and prediction plot



Results
The scatterplot suggested a roughly linear positive relationship between roller weight and lawn depression. The fitted regression model estimated a positive slope of about 2.67, indicating that heavier rollers tend to produce greater depression. The model had an R² of about 0.64, suggesting a moderate relationship between weight and depression.

title: Regression and Logistic Modeling in R
section: Part 2: Logistic Regression on Seed Germination
text:
Part 2: Logistic Regression on Seed Germination
This analysis investigates whether dilution level has a significant effect on the probability of seed germination.

Methods

Fit a grouped binomial logistic regression using cbind(y, n - y)
Tested whether dilution had a significant overall effect
Computed predicted probabilities and confidence intervals for each dilution level
Compared predicted germination probabilities across dilution groups



Results
The logistic regression results showed a highly significant overall effect of dilution on germination probability. Predicted probabilities suggested that the 1/1 dilution had much lower germination, while 1/25 and 1/625 had substantially higher and fairly similar germination probabilities. This indicates that dilution level strongly influences germination outcomes.

title: Regression and Logistic Modeling in R
text:
Visualization
The original assignment included scatterplots, fitted regression lines, prediction plots, and modeled probability curves supporting these conclusions.
View full analysis and code (PDF)

title: Regression and Logistic Modeling in R
text:
Key Takeaways

Linear regression can be used to model continuous outcomes and estimate fitted trends
Logistic regression is appropriate for grouped binomial outcomes such as germination success
Predicted values and confidence intervals help move from model output to practical interpretation

title: Survival Analysis of AML Patients Using Cox Proportional Hazards Model
text:
This project evaluates the effect of maintenance chemotherapy on remission duration in patients with Acute Myelogenous Leukemia (AML) using survival analysis techniques in R.
The analysis uses a randomized study of 23 patients comparing those who received maintenance chemotherapy with those who did not.

title: Survival Analysis of AML Patients Using Cox Proportional Hazards Model
text:
This project evaluates the effect of maintenance chemotherapy on remission duration in patients with Acute Myelogenous Leukemia (AML) using survival analysis techniques in R.
The analysis uses a randomized study of 23 patients comparing those who received maintenance chemotherapy with those who did not.

title: Survival Analysis of AML Patients Using Cox Proportional Hazards Model
text:
Data
The dataset includes:

time: duration of remission in weeks
cens: indicator of relapse (1 = relapse observed, 0 = censored)
treatment: maintenance chemotherapy (maint) or no treatment (none)

Censored observations represent patients for whom relapse was not observed during the study period, meaning their remission time is at least as long as recorded.

title: Survival Analysis of AML Patients Using Cox Proportional Hazards Model
text:
Methods

Constructed a survival object using Surv(time, cens == 1)
Fitted a Cox proportional hazards model to estimate treatment effects
Interpreted hazard ratios to compare relapse risk between groups
Generated predicted survival curves for each treatment group

title: Survival Analysis of AML Patients Using Cox Proportional Hazards Model
text:
Results
The Cox proportional hazards model produced a hazard ratio of approximately 2.50 for the no-treatment group relative to the maintenance chemotherapy group, indicating a substantially higher risk of relapse when no maintenance therapy is provided.
The corresponding two-sided p-value was approximately 0.0737. Because the research question was directional, a one-sided interpretation gives a p-value of approximately 0.037, which supports the conclusion that maintenance chemotherapy significantly prolongs remission time.
The survival curves support this conclusion by showing consistently higher probabilities of remaining in remission for the maintenance chemotherapy group across time.

title: Survival Analysis of AML Patients Using Cox Proportional Hazards Model
text:
Visualization
The survival curves showed that patients receiving maintenance chemotherapy had a higher probability of remaining in remission over time.
View full analysis and code (PDF)

title: Ordinal Logistic Regression on Legislative Support for SOPA
text:
This project uses ordinal logistic regression in R to analyze how legislators’ political party and campaign donations relate to their stance on the Stop Online Piracy Act (SOPA).
The response variable is ordinal, ranging from more negative positions on the bill to more positive ones, which makes ordinal logistic regression an appropriate modeling framework.

title: Ordinal Logistic Regression on Legislative Support for SOPA
text:
This project uses ordinal logistic regression in R to analyze how legislators’ political party and campaign donations relate to their stance on the Stop Online Piracy Act (SOPA).
The response variable is ordinal, ranging from more negative positions on the bill to more positive ones, which makes ordinal logistic regression an appropriate modeling framework.

title: Ordinal Logistic Regression on Legislative Support for SOPA
text:
Data
The dataset includes U.S. legislators and their recorded stance on SOPA, along with:

party: Democrat or Republican
money_pro: campaign contributions from groups supporting the bill
money_con: campaign contributions from groups opposing the bill
years: years of legislative service

Because the original stance variable included categories such as no, leaning no, undecided, yes, and unknown, the first step was to reorder the meaningful stance categories into a logical ordinal scale and remove observations with missing values.

title: Ordinal Logistic Regression on Legislative Support for SOPA
text:
Methods

Reordered the response variable into a sensible ordinal factor
Removed observations with missing values
Fit an ordinal logistic regression model using polr()
Used model comparison to assess whether all explanatory variables should remain in the model
Generated predicted probabilities for combinations of party and donation levels

title: Ordinal Logistic Regression on Legislative Support for SOPA
text:
Results
The initial model included party, pro-bill donations, anti-bill donations, and years of service. Model comparison showed that years could be removed without worsening fit, so the final model retained party, money_pro, and money_con.
The predicted probabilities showed a clear political pattern: holding donation levels constant, Democrats were more likely to be at the “yes” end of the scale, while Republicans were more likely to be at the “no” end.
The two donation variables also behaved as expected. Higher pro-bill donations increased the probability of a more supportive stance, while higher anti-bill donations shifted predicted probabilities toward opposition. In other words, legislators tended to move in the direction of the side contributing more money.

title: Ordinal Logistic Regression on Legislative Support for SOPA
text:
Visualization
The original assignment included predicted probability tables and a multi-panel prediction plot showing how stance probabilities changed across values of money_pro, party, and money_con.
View full analysis and code (PDF)

title: Ordinal Logistic Regression on Legislative Support for SOPA
text:
Key Takeaways

Ordinal logistic regression is appropriate when the response has more than two ordered categories
Model comparison helps identify variables that do not contribute meaningful explanatory power
Predicted probabilities are especially useful for interpreting ordinal models in substantive terms

title: Contact Information
text:
📧 Email: aktheexplorer@gmail.com


📞 Phone: +1 647-625-0780


🔗 LinkedIn: View Profile





👉 Download CV


I am particularly interested in how applied statistics can be used across a wide range of fields, especially finance, economics, politics, and the life sciences. More broadly, I am interested in the role statistical methods can play in research generally, and in how statistical tools can be used to make forecasts, draw meaningful inferences, and support deeper understanding across different kinds of fields.


I hope to apply my knowledge and skills in a professional setting where I can continue to grow as an individual while also making a meaningful contribution through my work. I am eager to use my background in a way that helps produce strong results, supports thoughtful decision-making, and contributes positively to society through careful analysis and responsible application of quantitative methods.

title: Alexandros Karagiannis
text:
Applied Statistics / Data Analysis & Visualization


Alma mater: University of Toronto Scarborough · Doukas School IB Department, Athens, Greece








I am a 4th year student at the University of Toronto Scarborough studying Applied Statistics, Economics, and Politics, with a strong interest in data science and the application of statistical thinking to real-world questions.
This portfolio brings together selected academic and analytical work in order to show both my technical skills and my broader interest in using quantitative methods to interpret data carefully, identify meaningful patterns, and draw well-grounded conclusions.

Ζητεῖν τὴν ἀλήθειαν
To seek the truth.
This idea reflects the motivation behind my work: to move beyond surface-level numbers, understand the deeper story data can tell, and apply statistical reasoning thoughtfully in order to reach meaningful judgments.

title: Assignment 5 Solution
text:
install.packages("marginaleffects")

Installing package into '/usr/local/lib/R/site-library'
(as 'lib' is unspecified)

library(tidyverse)

── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.5
✔ forcats   1.0.0     ✔ stringr   1.5.1
✔ ggplot2   3.5.0     ✔ tibble    3.2.1
✔ lubridate 1.9.3     ✔ tidyr     1.3.1
✔ purrr     1.0.2     


── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

library(marginaleffects)


my_url <- "http://datafiles.ritsokiguess.site/teak.csv"
teak <- read_csv(my_url)

Rows: 24 Columns: 4
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (3): Block, Plant, Root
dbl (1): Height

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

teak

# A tibble: 24 × 4
   Block Plant   Root  Height
   <chr> <chr>   <chr>  <dbl>
 1 I     pit     R4      18.2
 2 I     pit     R6      23.6
 3 I     pit     R8      24.8
 4 I     crowbar R4      12.7
 5 I     crowbar R6      15.4
 6 I     crowbar R8      16.8
 7 II    pit     R4      16.2
 8 II    pit     R6      22.7
 9 II    pit     R8      23.4
10 II    crowbar R4      11.9
# ℹ 14 more rows



ggplot(teak, aes(x = Root, y = Height, fill = Plant)) + geom_boxplot()




Both categorical variables are explanatory, but there are three levels of Root and only two of Plant , so its better to put Root on the x-axis and have only two colours for Plant .
Based on the boxplot when the planting method is Pit, the plant has a greater height on average than when the planting method is Crowbar, and the amount by which it is higher is about the same for each root length. Thus, it is expect to see a Plant effect that is the same for each root length: that is, no interaction between planting method and root length. To reaffirm our point we can draw an interaction plot and see whether it tells the same story as the boxplots. Note (it should, except for the fact that an interaction plot uses means and the boxplots use medians)

teak %>%
  group_by(Root, Plant) %>%
  summarize(mean_height = mean(Height)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Root, y = mean_height, colour = Plant, group = Plant)) +
geom_line()

`summarise()` has grouped output by 'Root'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.





Indeed those two traces do indeed look pretty close to parallel.

teak.1 <- aov(Height ~ Root * Plant, data = teak)
summary(teak.1)

            Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value   Pr(>F)    
Root         2 203.15  101.57  32.131 1.15e-06 ***
Plant        1 187.60  187.60  59.344 4.18e-07 ***
Root:Plant   2   3.78    1.89   0.598     0.56    
Residuals   18  56.90    3.16                     
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1


With a P-value of 0.56, this is nowhere near significant.

teak.2 <- aov(Height ~ Root + Plant, data = teak)
summary(teak.2)

            Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value   Pr(>F)    
Root         2 203.15  101.57   33.48 4.14e-07 ***
Plant        1 187.60  187.60   61.83 1.52e-07 ***
Residuals   20  60.68    3.03                     
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1


We can conclude that there are significant main effects, of root length (P-value 4.1 × 10−7) and of planting method (P-value 1.5 × 10−7). To find out what kind of effects they are, we run Tukey (which is necessary because there are three different root lengths):

TukeyHSD(teak.2)

  Tukey multiple comparisons of means
    95% family-wise confidence level

Fit: aov(formula = Height ~ Root + Plant, data = teak)

$Root
        diff      lwr     upr     p adj
R6-R4 5.6875  3.48403 7.89097 0.0000067
R8-R4 6.5625  4.35903 8.76597 0.0000008
R8-R6 0.8750 -1.32847 3.07847 0.5825209

$Plant
                diff      lwr      upr p adj
pit-crowbar 5.591667 4.108292 7.075041 2e-07


The Plant analysis here doesn’t tell us anything we haven’t already seen: when the Pit planting method is used, the height is on average greater than for the Crowbar method. The Root analysis, however, does tell us something new: the height for plants that had root length 4 inches is significantly less than for plants with either of the other two root lengths (which are not significantly different). There is no value in doing simple effects here, because the interaction is not significant.

my_url <- "http://datafiles.ritsokiguess.site/rhiz-clover.csv"
clover <- read_csv(my_url)

Rows: 60 Columns: 5
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (2): comb, strain
dbl (3): weight, nitro, Npg

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

clover

# A tibble: 60 × 5
   comb   strain weight nitro   Npg
   <chr>  <chr>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
 1 clover 3DOk1    1.02  31.2  30.6
 2 clover 3DOk1    0.65  23.6  36.3
 3 clover 3DOk1    0.55  14    25.5
 4 clover 3DOk1    0.86  23.1  26.9
 5 clover 3DOk1    0.9   23.4  26  
 6 clover 3DOk5    1.18  59    50  
 7 clover 3DOk5    1.3   36.7  28.2
 8 clover 3DOk5    0.86  38.7  45  
 9 clover 3DOk5    1.62  47.3  29.2
10 clover 3DOk5    0.9   26.1  29  
# ℹ 50 more rows


There are correctly 12 × 5 = 60 observations, and the variables of interest are as described. (The value
of Npg is weight divided by nitro , which is why it has a lot of decimals.)

clover %>%
  group_by(comb, strain) %>%
  summarize(mean_npg = mean(Npg)) -> clover_means

`summarise()` has grouped output by 'comb'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.

clover_means

# A tibble: 12 × 3
# Groups:   comb [2]
   comb           strain mean_npg
   <chr>          <chr>     <dbl>
 1 clover         3DOk1      29.0
 2 clover         3DOk13     22.5
 3 clover         3DOk4      21.4
 4 clover         3DOk5      36.3
 5 clover         3DOk7      22.9
 6 clover         k.comp     26.0
 7 clover+alfalfa 3DOk1      28.4
 8 clover+alfalfa 3DOk13     24.3
 9 clover+alfalfa 3DOk4      24.0
10 clover+alfalfa 3DOk5      27.4
11 clover+alfalfa 3DOk7      25.0
12 clover+alfalfa k.comp     24.9



ggplot(clover_means, aes(x = strain, y = mean_npg,
                         colour = comb, group = comb)) + geom_line()




This is a plot of the mean value of Npg (y-axis) for each combination of comb and strain . One of the explanatory variables goes on the x-axis, and the other is colour.
The major reason for drawing an interaction plot is to see whether we have an interaction, and that’s assessed by making a call about whether the lines on your plot are parallel. Here, it seems clear that they are not: the strain 3DOk5 seems very different from the others. So we would expect to see an interaction.

clover.1 <- aov(Npg ~ comb * strain, data = clover)
summary(clover.1)

            Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value   Pr(>F)    
comb         1    6.9    6.88   0.531  0.46955    
strain       5  642.3  128.45   9.916 1.47e-06 ***
comb:strain  5  228.2   45.65   3.524  0.00857 ** 
Residuals   48  621.8   12.95                     
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1


The interaction is significant (which is our finding from the ANOVA), so a suitable technique would be to use simple effects to understand what that significant interaction actually means.

clover %>%
  filter(strain == "3DOk7") -> d1
d1.1 <- aov(Npg ~ comb, data = d1)
summary(d1.1)

            Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)  
comb         1 10.322  10.322   10.51 0.0118 *
Residuals    8  7.854   0.982                 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1


There is a significant difference in nitrogen content between the two cultures of 3DOk7. To see which way around they are, look back at the interaction plot to see that the nitrogen content of the pure clover culture is lower than for the clover plus alfalfa culture. Or, we can perform Tukey (which is otherwise pointless) to see the same thing:

TukeyHSD(d1.1)

  Tukey multiple comparisons of means
    95% family-wise confidence level

Fit: aov(formula = Npg ~ comb, data = d1)

$comb
                          diff      lwr      upr     p adj
clover+alfalfa-clover 2.031939 0.586832 3.477045 0.0118345


The mean nitrogen content for clover plus alfalfa is higher than for just clover (by between 0.5 and 3.5 units). Tukey is otherwise pointless here because there are only two levels of comb (two cultures of this strain), and we know they are significantly different.

clover %>%
  filter(strain == "3DOk1") -> d2
d2.1 <- aov(Npg ~ comb, data = d2)
summary(d2.1)

            Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)
comb         1   1.01   1.013    0.09  0.772
Residuals    8  90.12  11.265               


This is not significant
The ANOVA earlier found a significant interaction. This means that the effect of comb will be different for the different strains. In particular, we found from the simple effects analyses that there is no effect of comb for strain 3DOk1 , but there is an effect for strain 3DOk7 : that is, the effect of comb depends on which strain you are looking at.

title: Two Way ANOVA on rhizobium nitrogen & on teak tree growth
text: This project combines two statistical analyses completed in R. The first uses two-way analysis of variance to examine how planting method and root length affect teak tree height after one year of growth. The second analyzes nitrogen fixation in clover plants using a two-way ANOVA with interaction to evaluate whether rhizobium strain and mixed bacterial culture influence nitrogen content.

title: Two Way ANOVA on rhizobium nitrogen & on teak tree growth
text: This project combines two statistical analyses completed in R. The first uses two-way analysis of variance to examine how planting method and root length affect teak tree height after one year of growth. The second analyzes nitrogen fixation in clover plants using a two-way ANOVA with interaction to evaluate whether rhizobium strain and mixed bacterial culture influence nitrogen content.

title: Two Way ANOVA on rhizobium nitrogen & on teak tree growth
text:
Part 1: Teak Growth Analysis
This analysis investigates whether planting method, root length at planting, and their combination affect teak tree height after one year.

title: Two Way ANOVA on rhizobium nitrogen & on teak tree growth
text:
Data
The teak dataset includes 24 observations and records:

Plant: planting method, either crowbar or pit
Root: root length at planting, recorded as R4, R6, or R8
Height: plant height after one year in inches

An additional blocking variable, Block, was present in the dataset but was not required for the main analysis.

title: Two Way ANOVA on rhizobium nitrogen & on teak tree growth
text:
Methods

Read in and displayed the teak dataset
Created grouped boxplots of Height by Root and Plant
Assessed the possibility of interaction visually using grouped boxplots and an interaction plot
Fit a two-way ANOVA with interaction
Removed the non-significant interaction and refit the model
Used Tukey comparisons to determine which root-length groups differed

title: Two Way ANOVA on rhizobium nitrogen & on teak tree growth
text:
Results
The grouped boxplots suggested that pit planting produced taller plants on average than crowbar planting, and that this difference was fairly consistent across root lengths. This indicated little evidence of an interaction. The two-way ANOVA confirmed that the interaction between Root and Plant was not significant, while both main effects were strongly significant. Follow-up comparisons showed that plants with root length 4 inches were significantly shorter than those with root lengths 6 or 8 inches, while 6 and 8 inches did not differ significantly. The pit method also produced greater average height than the crowbar method.

title: Two Way ANOVA on rhizobium nitrogen & on teak tree growth
text:
Part 2: Rhizobium Nitrogen Fixation Analysis
This analysis studies whether nitrogen content in clover plants depends on rhizobium strain, bacterial culture combination, and the interaction between them.

title: Two Way ANOVA on rhizobium nitrogen & on teak tree growth
text:
Data
The rhizobium dataset includes 60 observations and records:

strain: one of six clover rhizobium cultures
comb: whether the culture was pure clover or clover mixed with alfalfa rhizobium
Npg: milligrams of nitrogen per gram of dry plant weight

Five observations were collected for each strain and culture combination.

title: Two Way ANOVA on rhizobium nitrogen & on teak tree growth
text:
Methods

Read in and displayed the rhizobium dataset
Calculated treatment means and created an interaction plot
Interpreted the interaction plot to assess whether the traces were parallel
Fit a two-way ANOVA with interaction
Used simple effects to examine the effect of comb for selected strains
Compared results for strain 3DOk7 and strain 3DOk1

title: Two Way ANOVA on rhizobium nitrogen & on teak tree growth
text:
Results
The interaction plot suggested that the effect of comb differed across strains, especially for strain 3DOk5, so an interaction was expected. The two-way ANOVA confirmed a significant comb:strain interaction. Because of that interaction, simple effects were used to understand the pattern more clearly. For strain 3DOk7, the effect of comb was significant, with clover plus alfalfa producing higher nitrogen content than clover alone. For strain 3DOk1, there was no significant effect of comb. These results are consistent with the earlier ANOVA result, since a significant interaction implies that the effect of bacterial combination depends on the strain being considered.

title: Two Way ANOVA on rhizobium nitrogen & on teak tree growth
text:
Visualization
The original assignment included grouped boxplots, an interaction plot for teak, an interaction plot for rhizobium, and simple-effects follow-up analyses supporting the conclusions.
View full analysis and code (PDF)

title: Two Way ANOVA on rhizobium nitrogen & on teak tree growth
text:
Key Takeaways

Two-way ANOVA is useful for testing both main effects and interactions between explanatory variables
When the interaction is not significant, the analysis can continue by interpreting the main effects
When the interaction is significant, simple effects help explain how the relationship changes across groups
Visual tools such as grouped boxplots and interaction plots are valuable for anticipating the structure of ANOVA results

title: Assignment 6 Solution
text:
install.packages("car")

Installing package into '/usr/local/lib/R/site-library'
(as 'lib' is unspecified)

library(tidyverse)

── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.5
✔ forcats   1.0.0     ✔ stringr   1.5.1
✔ ggplot2   3.5.0     ✔ tibble    3.2.1
✔ lubridate 1.9.3     ✔ tidyr     1.3.1
✔ purrr     1.0.2

✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

library(car)

Loading required package: carData

Attaching package: 'car'

The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr':

    recode

The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':

    some



my_url <- "http://datafiles.ritsokiguess.site/fact-opinion.csv"
survey <- read_csv(my_url)

Rows: 5035 Columns: 2
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (1): age
dbl (1): correct

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

survey

# A tibble: 5,035 × 2
   age   correct
   <chr>   <dbl>
 1 50-65       6
 2 65+         5
 3 28-38       9
 4 65+         8
 5 18-27       9
 6 18-27      10
 7 18-27       7
 8 28-38       5
 9 65+         9
10 28-38      10
# ℹ 5,025 more rows



survey %>% mutate(age = factor(age)) -> survey
survey

# A tibble: 5,035 × 2
   age   correct
   <fct>   <dbl>
 1 50-65       6
 2 65+         5
 3 28-38       9
 4 65+         8
 5 18-27       9
 6 18-27      10
 7 18-27       7
 8 28-38       5
 9 65+         9
10 28-38      10
# ℹ 5,025 more rows



youngest <- c(1, -1, 0, 0, 0)
oldest <- c(0, 0, 0, 1, -1)
young_vs_middle <- c(1/2, 1/2, -1, 0, 0)
young_vs_old <- c(1/3, 1/3, 1/3, -1/2, -1/2)

First thing we make sure is that the five numbers within each contrast add up to zero (this is actually the definition of a contrast). The second thing to worry about is that the number on the bottom of a fraction (in the last two contrasts) represents the number of means in a group we are comparing. For example, for the last one, we are comparing the three youngest groups (so the weight for each of these should be 1/3) against the two oldest groups (so the weight for each of those should be 1/2 with the opposite sign).
The order of the numbers in each contrast is the same as the order of the age groups that you found with count above: that is, the first number corresponds to the 18–27 age group, and so if it zero, it means that the 18–27 age group does not figure in that contrast.
Alternatives: We can multiply a contrast through by any constant, which could be −1 (which will flip all the signs), or by something that will make the weights whole numbers (eg., 6 for the last one, so that the weights become 2 three times followed by −3 twice). Doing this won’t stop any contrast from being a contrast (the five weights will still add up to zero) and won’t change the orthogonal-ness of any contrasts.

young_vs_middle * young_vs_old

[1]  0.1666667  0.1666667 -0.3333333  0.0000000  0.0000000



sum(young_vs_middle * young_vs_old)

[1] 0



m <- cbind(youngest, oldest, young_vs_middle, young_vs_old)
contrasts(survey$age) <- m
survey.1 <- lm(correct ~ age, data = survey)

m

     youngest oldest young_vs_middle young_vs_old
[1,]        1      0             0.5    0.3333333
[2,]       -1      0             0.5    0.3333333
[3,]        0      0            -1.0    0.3333333
[4,]        0      1             0.0   -0.5000000
[5,]        0     -1             0.0   -0.5000000


Three steps:
glue the contrasts together into a matrix
set that as contrasts for your factor column
run the ANOVA as if it is a regression

Each column has the name of its contrast, and the contrast weights can be read down the columns. The purpose of the contrasts line is to set lm up so that the “regression slopes” become the contrasts we want to test.
A more mathematical way to check for orthogonality is to work out m -transpose matrix-multiplied by m:

t(m) %*% m

                youngest oldest young_vs_middle young_vs_old
youngest               2      0             0.0    0.0000000
oldest                 0      2             0.0    0.0000000
young_vs_middle        0      0             1.5    0.0000000
young_vs_old           0      0             0.0    0.8333333


This is a shorthand way of checking all the contrasts for orthogonality with each other. Ignore the numbers down the diagonal (top-left to bottom-right), since a contrast is not going to be orthogonal with itself; as long as all the off-diagonal numbers are zero, the contrasts are orthogonal.

summary(survey.1)


Call:
lm(formula = correct ~ age, data = survey)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-7.5186 -1.6635  0.3104  1.5219  3.3365 

Coefficients:
                    Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)         7.171768   0.032520 220.537   <2e-16 ***
ageyoungest         0.004817   0.057243   0.084    0.933    
ageoldest          -0.013085   0.039719  -0.329    0.742    
ageyoung_vs_middle  0.023785   0.067651   0.352    0.725    
ageyoung_vs_old     0.990442   0.074109  13.365   <2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 2.157 on 5030 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.03439,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.03362 
F-statistic: 44.78 on 4 and 5030 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16


the two youngest age groups do not differ significantly (in ability to detect fact from opinion in news statements)
the two oldest age groups do not differ significantly (ditto)
the two youngest age groups (taken together) do not differ significantly from the 39–49 age group the three youngest age groups do differ significantly (in detecting fact from opinion in news stories) from the two oldest age groups.

ggplot(survey, aes(x = age, y = correct)) + geom_boxplot()




There seems to be a sharp dividing line at age 50, with respondents older than that doing a worse job on average at distinguishing fact from opinion than younger respondents. The boxplot, in fact, tells exactly the same story as the contrasts, but the contrasts come with P-values attached. The sample size is large (over 600 observations in each group), so that even though the above-50 vs. below-50 difference is small, it is very definitely significant.

my_url <- "http://datafiles.ritsokiguess.site/onions.csv"
onions <- read_csv(my_url)

Rows: 84 Columns: 3
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (1): location
dbl (2): dens, yield

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

onions

# A tibble: 84 × 3
    dens yield location       
   <dbl> <dbl> <chr>          
 1  23.5  223. Purnong Landing
 2  26.2  234. Purnong Landing
 3  27.8  222. Purnong Landing
 4  32.9  222. Purnong Landing
 5  33.3  197. Purnong Landing
 6  36.8  190. Purnong Landing
 7  37.6  211. Purnong Landing
 8  37.6  191. Purnong Landing
 9  41.5  157. Purnong Landing
10  42.7  168. Purnong Landing
# ℹ 74 more rows



ggplot(onions, aes(x = dens, y = yield,
                   colour = location)) +
  geom_point() + geom_smooth()

`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'





These are downward but non-linear trends: they descend quickly at first and then level off.

library(MASS, exclude = "select")


Attaching package: 'MASS'


The following object is masked _by_ '.GlobalEnv':

    survey



boxcox(yield ~ dens * location, data = onions)




Inside the boxcox , we put the best model that we know of at the moment (including the interaction, because we don’t know yet whether it can be removed). (That said, the decision is the same if we leave it out on this occasion, but the right thing to do is to put the interaction in “just in case”.)
The best transformation appears to be power −0.5, that is, one over square root, which is in the confidence interval, supporting what the experimenters said.
The second-best way to do it is to re-draw the plot of the previous question, but with yield replaced with one over the square root of yield, and demonstrate that the trends are now straight, or close to it. Once again, use a default smooth rather than a regression line (because we want to see whether the trends are straight, not to assume that they are at this point).

ggplot(onions, aes(x = dens, y = 1 / sqrt(yield),
                   colour = location)) +
  geom_point() + geom_smooth()

`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'





The trends are at least straighter than they were before, but this does not rule out the possibility that a better transformation could be found (whereas the Box-Cox narrows things down a lot, ruling out the log and reciprocal transformations).

onions.1 <- lm(1 / sqrt(yield) ~ dens * location, data = onions)

or raise yield to a negative power using ^ , but then its important to remember that ^ has a special meaning in model formulas, so we need to wrap the power in I() to work around that.

onions.1 <- lm(I(yield^(-0.5)) ~ dens * location, data = onions)

Then, to see whether we can get rid of the interaction, the best way is this (with an F -test, because it is an ordinary regression):

drop1(onions.1, test = "F")

Single term deletions

Model:
I(yield^(-0.5)) ~ dens * location
              Df Sum of Sq       RSS     AIC F value   Pr(>F)    
<none>                     0.0023146 -873.94                     
dens:location  1 0.0010544 0.0033690 -844.41  36.443 4.67e-08 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1


and we see that the interaction, with a P-value of 4.7 × 10−8, much less than 0.05, has to stay.

summary(onions.1)


Call:
lm(formula = I(yield^(-0.5)) ~ dens * location, data = onions)

Residuals:
       Min         1Q     Median         3Q        Max 
-0.0121384 -0.0031970 -0.0006592  0.0034537  0.0128670 

Coefficients:
                       Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)           5.673e-02  1.781e-03  31.859  < 2e-16 ***
dens                  4.667e-04  1.992e-05  23.425  < 2e-16 ***
locationVirginia      4.001e-03  2.412e-03   1.659    0.101    
dens:locationVirginia 1.734e-04  2.873e-05   6.037 4.67e-08 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.005379 on 80 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.9519,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9501 
F-statistic: 527.6 on 3 and 80 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16


and it happens that the P-value for the interaction is the same. It worked in this case because there were only two locations, and so comparing the slope for Virginia with that of the baseline location Purnong Landing is actually doing the same test. (If there were three locations, it would be a different story.)

ggplot(onions, aes(x = dens, y = 1/sqrt(yield), colour = factor(location))) +
  geom_point() + geom_smooth(method = "lm")

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'






ggplot(onions, aes(x = dens, y = yield^(-0.5), colour = factor(location))) +
  geom_point() + geom_smooth(method = "lm")

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'





The interaction was significant because those lines are not parallel.

my_url <- "http://datafiles.ritsokiguess.site/mercer-wheat.csv"
wheat <- read_csv(my_url)

Rows: 500 Columns: 4
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (2): row, col
dbl (2): grain, straw

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

wheat

# A tibble: 500 × 4
   row   col   grain straw
   <chr> <chr> <dbl> <dbl>
 1 E     J      3.63  6.37
 2 D     J      4.07  6.24
 3 C     J      4.51  7.05
 4 B     J      3.9   6.91
 5 A     J      3.63  5.93
 6 E     J      3.16  5.59
 7 D     J      3.18  5.32
 8 C     J      3.42  5.52
 9 B     J      3.97  6.03
10 A     J      3.4   5.66
# ℹ 490 more rows


There are two (quantitative) response variables (the two variables that were measured), namely grain and straw.

wheat.1  <-  manova(cbind(grain, straw) ~ row * col, data = wheat)
summary(wheat.1)

           Df  Pillai approx F num Df den Df    Pr(>F)    
row         4 0.22035   14.704      8    950 < 2.2e-16 ***
col         4 0.21046   13.966      8    950 < 2.2e-16 ***
row:col    16 0.51195   10.214     32    950 < 2.2e-16 ***
Residuals 475                                             
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1



wheat.2 <- lm(cbind(grain, straw) ~ row * col, data = wheat)
Manova(wheat.2)


Type II MANOVA Tests: Pillai test statistic
        Df test stat approx F num Df den Df    Pr(>F)    
row      4   0.22035   14.704      8    950 < 2.2e-16 ***
col      4   0.21046   13.966      8    950 < 2.2e-16 ***
row:col 16   0.51195   10.214     32    950 < 2.2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1


The strategy here is to fit a “multivariate regression” using lm with your two-column response variable, and then to display the Manova (uppercase M) of that.5 The result, including the test statistics and degrees of freedom, is the same, so either way is good.
The interaction is significant, so one or both of the amount of grain or straw depends on the combination of row and col in some way.

ggplot(wheat, aes(x = grain, y = straw, colour = row, shape = col)) + geom_point()




We are in the happy position of having two response variables rather than more, so we can make a non- three-dimensional graph. Let’s summarize our variables:
grain : quantitative, response straw : quantitative, response row : categorical, explanatory col : categorical, explanatory.
If we only had one categorical variable, we could draw a scatterplot with points identified by colour. The best way here is to find out how to distinguish the other categorical variable by something else other than colour, such as plotting symbol (that is, plot the points using different symbols rather than just circles).
The points at the bottom right seem to be different from the others (off the trend of the others), and they are all light blue plus signs. Look at the legends to the right to see that they are all from row D (light blue) and column M (plus signs).
Because it’s a combination of row and column, this shows up as a significant interaction in the analysis. It’s not true, for example, that row D is always different from the other rows (which would be a row D main effect); it’s only different when combined with column M.

title: Contrasts, Transformations & MANOVA
text: This project combines three statistical analyses completed in R. The first uses custom contrasts to compare age groups in their ability to distinguish factual from opinion-based news statements. The second models onion yield as a function of planting density and location, using a response transformation and interaction term. The third uses multivariate analysis of variance to study wheat grain and straw production across rows and columns in a field trial.

title: Contrasts, Transformations & MANOVA
text: This project combines three statistical analyses completed in R. The first uses custom contrasts to compare age groups in their ability to distinguish factual from opinion-based news statements. The second models onion yield as a function of planting density and location, using a response transformation and interaction term. The third uses multivariate analysis of variance to study wheat grain and straw production across rows and columns in a field trial.

title: Contrasts, Transformations & MANOVA
section: Part 1: Fact vs Opinion Classification by Age Group
text:
Part 1: Fact vs Opinion Classification by Age Group
This analysis investigates whether different age groups vary in the number of news statements they correctly classify as fact or opinion.

title: Contrasts, Transformations & MANOVA
text:
Data
The dataset contains 5,035 observations and records:

age: age group of the respondent
correct: number of statements correctly classified out of ten

The age groups were converted to a factor so that contrasts could be assigned and tested properly.

title: Contrasts, Transformations & MANOVA
text:
Methods

Read in and displayed the survey data
Converted age into a factor
Defined four planned contrasts:

18–27 vs 28–38
50–65 vs 65+
average of 18–27 and 28–38 vs 39–49
average of the three youngest groups vs average of the two oldest groups

Verified orthogonality for selected contrasts
Set the contrast matrix in the factor and fit a linear model

title: Contrasts, Transformations & MANOVA
text:
Results
The first three contrasts were not significant, meaning that the youngest two groups did not differ from each other, the oldest two groups did not differ from each other, and the two youngest groups together did not differ from the 39–49 group. The final contrast was significant, showing that the three youngest age groups performed differently from the two oldest groups in distinguishing fact from opinion. This suggests a broad age divide rather than isolated pairwise differences.

title: Contrasts, Transformations & MANOVA
section: Part 2: Onion Yield and Planting Density
text:
Part 2: Onion Yield and Planting Density
This analysis studies how onion yield per plant depends on planting density and whether the relationship differs by location.

title: Contrasts, Transformations & MANOVA
text:
Data
The onion dataset contains 84 observations and records:

dens: planting density
yield: onion yield in grams per plant
location: field location in South Australia

title: Contrasts, Transformations & MANOVA
text:
Methods

Read in and displayed the onion data
Drew scatterplots with smooth trends by location
Showed that the relationship between yield and density was non-linear
Used a Box-Cox procedure to support transforming the response as 1 / sqrt(yield)
Fit a linear model with transformed response and interaction
Tested whether the interaction term should remain
Graphed the transformed response to interpret the interaction

title: Contrasts, Transformations & MANOVA
text:
Results
The original relationship between yield and density was downward and non-linear for both locations. The Box-Cox analysis supported using the inverse square root transformation, which made the trends more linear. After fitting the transformed model, the density-by-location interaction was highly significant and needed to remain in the model. This was not surprising because the transformed regression lines for the two locations were not parallel. On the original scale, Purnong Landing appeared to produce better yield across planting densities, although the size of the difference varied with density.

title: Contrasts, Transformations & MANOVA
section: Part 3: MANOVA for Wheat Grain and Straw
text:
Part 3: MANOVA for Wheat Grain and Straw
This analysis examines whether wheat grain and straw production differ across field rows, columns, and their interaction.

title: Contrasts, Transformations & MANOVA
text:
Data
The wheat dataset contains 500 observations and records:

row: row label of the field
col: column label of the field
grain: amount of grain in pounds
straw: amount of straw in pounds

There were 20 observations for each of the 25 row-column field combinations.

title: Contrasts, Transformations & MANOVA
text:
Methods

Read in and displayed the wheat data
Identified grain and straw as the two quantitative response variables
Fit a multivariate analysis of variance with row, col, and row:col
Interpreted the MANOVA results using Pillai’s trace
Used scatterplots and faceted visualizations to identify which row-column combination differed from the others

title: Contrasts, Transformations & MANOVA
text:
Results
The MANOVA showed significant effects for row, col, and especially the row:col interaction. Because the interaction was significant, the main effects should not be interpreted on their own. The follow-up graphs showed that the unusual combination was field D-M, which stood apart from the rest in the joint relationship between grain and straw. This field produced a noticeably different pattern than the other row-column combinations, which explains the significant interaction.

title: Contrasts, Transformations & MANOVA
text:
Visualization
The original assignment included boxplots for age-group comparisons, smoothed and transformed scatterplots for onions, a Box-Cox plot, and several visualizations for the wheat MANOVA, including colour-shape scatterplots and faceted displays.
View full analysis and code (PDF)

title: Contrasts, Transformations & MANOVA
text:
Key Takeaways

Planned contrasts allow specific group comparisons to be tested directly and independently
Box-Cox analysis is useful for identifying response transformations that improve linear modeling
A significant interaction means that the effect of one explanatory variable depends on the level of another
MANOVA is appropriate when there are multiple quantitative response variables that may vary jointly
Graphs are essential for explaining significant interactions and identifying which combinations drive them

title: Alexandros Karagiannis
Alexandros Karagiannis
text:
I am a 4th year student at the University of Toronto Scarborough studying Applied Statistics, Economics, and Politics, with a strong interest in data science and the application of statistical thinking to real-world questions.
This portfolio brings together selected academic and analytical work in order to show both my technical skills and my broader interest in using quantitative methods to interpret data carefully, identify meaningful patterns, and draw well-grounded conclusions.

Ζητεῖν τὴν ἀλήθειαν
To seek the truth.
This idea reflects the motivation behind my work: to move beyond surface-level numbers, understand the deeper story data can tell, and apply statistical reasoning thoughtfully in order to reach meaningful judgments.
